era_context: The Ur III dynasty in Mesopotamia is in its final decades under Ibbi-Sin, facing severe external pressure from Elamites and Amorites. Egypt is fragmented in the First Intermediate Period. The Indus Valley Civilization is at its mature phase. Traditional Chinese historiography places the Xia dynasty in its early-to-middle period, though archaeological confirmation is debated. The Bronze Age is well-established; long-distance trade networks connect Mesopotamia, the Indus Valley, the Persian Gulf, and Central Asia.

events.title: Ibbi-Sin's reign continues amid Ur III state decline
events.description: Ibbi-Sin (r. c. 2028–2004 BCE) continues to rule the Ur III state, but his authority is increasingly nominal. Elamite forces under Kindattu press from the east, and Amorite groups infiltrate from the west. Cuneiform administrative texts show further erosion of central control. The state's economic resources are strained by military expenditures and the loss of tributary territories. Within a few years, Ibbi-Sin will be captured by the Elamites, ending the Ur III dynasty and the last unified Sumerian state.

sources.name: Ur III cuneiform administrative texts and king lists

events.certainty_note: Ibbi-Sin's reign is well-attested in cuneiform sources. Absolute dating to -2007 relies on conventional Mesopotamian chronology (±50 years). The year falls within his reign with high probability.

events.title: Harappan civilization: mature phase continues
events.region: Indus Valley (modern Pakistan and northwest India)
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization remains at its mature phase, with major urban centers (Mohenjo-daro, Harappa, Dholavira, Lothal) functioning as centers of trade, craft production, and administration. Standardized weights, measures, and seals continue to facilitate economic activity. Long-distance trade with Mesopotamia and Central Asia is well-established. The undeciphered Indus script appears on seals and pottery, suggesting a complex administrative or religious system. No major disruptions are evident at this time.
events.certainty_note: The Harappan civilization's mature phase is well-established archaeologically. The period c. 2600–1900 BCE is well-dated; -2007 falls within this period with high confidence.

disconfirming_evidence: No significant disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. The chronology of the Ur III collapse and the Harappan civilization remains subject to scholarly debate regarding absolute dating, but the general historical narratives are well-supported.
historiographic_note: Documentation for -2007 is sparse. Mesopotamian cuneiform sources are abundant but administrative in nature. The Indus Valley is known only through archaeology and undeciphered inscriptions. Egypt is known through later sources and archaeology. China is known only through traditional historiography. Large regions of the world are absent from the historical record.

graph_edges.note: Ibbi-Sin's reign leads to the fall of Ur III
